talk_title: ARCH-DR multimodal single-cell profiling of esophageal adenocarcinoma

speaker.name: Nick Navin
speaker.affiliation: University of California, San Francisco, Department of Pathology

MOA_summary: ARCH-DR is a high‑throughput archival DNA‑RNA co‑sequencing chemistry that simultaneously captures copy‑number alterations, somatic mutations and full‑length transcriptomes from thousands of single cells in fresh, frozen or FFPE esophageal adenocarcinoma specimens, enabling reconstruction of genetic lineages and phenotypic plasticity.
key_findings: Analyzed 12 EAC patients (≈34,000 cells) with ARCH‑DR, revealing patient‑specific transcriptional clusters driven by copy‑number dosage effects.
Identified three epithelial lineages (esophageal, gastric, intestinal) within individual tumors, with most subclones displaying mixed lineage phenotypes, indicating high cellular plasticity.
Detected ecDNA amplifications of EGFR and ERBB2 that segregated with distinct genetic subclones and showed expression saturation at ~20 copies.
Found that ~51% of genes exhibit dosage‑sensitive expression correlating with copy‑number changes, while the remainder are dosage‑insensitive.
Observed that ~20% of somatic mutations are associated with altered gene expression, highlighting mutation‑driven transcriptional effects.
open_questions: What molecular mechanisms underlie the observed dosage‑insensitive genes in EAC?
Do specific ecDNA events influence therapeutic response or resistance?
How do non‑cancer stromal cells acquire chromosome X/Y losses and what functional impact do they have on tumor immunity?

talk_title: PTA enables uniform single‑cell genome amplification and reveals hidden mutational diversity in pediatric leukemia

models.name: pediatric leukemia cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: multiple diagnostic and post‑treatment samples, single‑cell WGS (2,000‑4,000 mutations per cell)

clinical_data.value: detected in >90% of post‑treatment resistant clones

MOA_summary: Primary Template-directed Amplification (PTA) adds irreversible terminators to a Phi29‑based whole‑genome amplification, producing uniformly covered single‑cell genomes that retain accurate allele frequencies, enabling high‑sensitivity SNV and copy‑number detection, which was applied to pediatric leukemia to uncover a hidden mutational burden and clonal drug‑resistance mechanisms.
key_findings: PTA achieves ~90% detection of known germline variants with allele‑frequency distribution centered at 50%, outperforming MDA and PCR‑based methods.
Single‑cell sequencing of pediatric leukemia revealed 2,000–4,000 mutations per cell, far exceeding the <1,000 mutations observed in bulk sequencing.
De‑novo mutational signatures (SBS2/13 APOBEC) were identified at the single‑cell level, showing heterogeneous signature activity across clones.
Post‑treatment MRD analysis uncovered JAK2‑mutant subclones that were undetectable in bulk but expanded after chemotherapy, suggesting a genetic basis for resistance.
Integration of surface‑marker index sorting with PTA enabled mapping of phenotypic (surface protein) and genotypic (SNV/CNV) traits onto phylogenetic trees.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: JAK2‑mutant clones were selectively enriched after chemotherapy, indicating a potential resistance driver.
open_questions: Can PTA‑derived mutational signatures predict therapeutic response in real time?
What additional genomic events cooperate with JAK2 mutations to drive MRD persistence?

talk_title: SKY‑Met: high‑throughput single‑cell DNA methylation and multi‑omic profiling

speaker.name: Andrew Addy
speaker.affiliation: Oregon Health & Science University, Department of Molecular Medicine

models.name: AML cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: single‑cell methylome + ATAC co‑assay from bone‑marrow samples

MOA_summary: SKY‑Met combines combinatorial indexing with nucleosome disruption to achieve genome‑wide bisulfite‑free single‑cell DNA methylation profiling at high throughput; the platform also supports simultaneous ATAC‑seq or whole‑genome sequencing, enabling multi‑omic interrogation of epigenetic state, chromatin accessibility and copy‑number in thousands of cells from frozen or fixed tissues.
key_findings: Generated a 140,000‑cell methylome atlas of the human cerebral cortex, revealing cell‑type‑specific CpG methylation patterns and non‑CG methylation dynamics in neuronal subtypes.
Applied SKY‑Met to pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, achieving >90% CpG coverage per cell and enabling copy‑number profiling from frozen tumor specimens.
Demonstrated a joint methylome‑ATAC co‑assay on ~8% of 140,000 bone‑marrow cells, providing concordant cell‑type clustering from both modalities.
Detected a balanced translocation in a PDAC subclone using combined single‑cell genome + Hi‑C reads, illustrating the ability to capture structural variants alongside epigenetic information.

combination_strategies.rationale: to link chromatin accessibility with DNA methylation in the same cell
combination_strategies.phenotype: concordant cell‑type clustering from both modalities

combination_strategies.rationale: to capture 3D genome architecture alongside methylation

open_questions: How can methylation‑based cell‑type markers be optimized for tumor sub‑clustering where CG methylation is less discriminative?
What is the functional impact of non‑CG methylation observed in rare tumor‑associated neuronal‑like cells?
